title: Manufacturing and Inherent Discontinuities
narrationScript: Every discontinuity has a birthplace, and a Level Two inspector who knows the birthplaces can predict indications before the part is even on the bench. That is the skill we build in this lesson. We are going to trace discontinuities back to three origins. Inherent discontinuities that form when the original ingot solidifies. Primary manufacturing discontinuities that form when metal is cast to shape. And secondary manufacturing discontinuities that form when sound metal is forged or rolled into product. Start with the melt. When molten steel solidifies in an ingot mold, it shrinks. The last metal to freeze sits at the top center, and when it runs out of liquid to feed that shrinkage, a funnel shaped cavity forms. We call it pipe. Dissolved gases come out of solution as the metal freezes and leave rounded holes, which is gas porosity. Bits of slag, refractory, and oxide get trapped as nonmetallic inclusions. And alloying elements concentrate in the last regions to freeze, which we call segregation. Mills crop the worst of this off the top of the ingot, but whatever survives travels into every product made from that ingot. Here is the part to remember. When the ingot is rolled or forged, those inherent discontinuities change shape. Pipe and porosity get flattened into laminations in plate. Inclusions get stretched into stringers. Ingot surface defects get rolled into tight seams. So when you find a lamination on a plate edge, you are really looking at an ingot problem that is years downstream. Now castings, which the outline treats as primary manufacturing. A casting freezes in its final shape, so everything depends on how the liquid metal flowed and cooled. Gas porosity shows up as smooth, round holes with shiny walls. Shrinkage looks completely different. It is jagged, spongy, and dendritic, and it lives where the metal froze last. That means heavy sections, junctions where walls meet, and right under risers. A cold shut forms where two streams of metal met after their fronts had cooled too much to fuse. It looks like a smooth, curved line with rounded edges, almost like a fold in the surface, and you find it on thin walls far from the gate, where the metal arrives coldest. A hot tear is the opposite personality. It forms while the casting is still hot and weak, when the mold restrains shrinkage, and it looks ragged and branched, usually at a change in section thickness or a restrained corner. Sand and slag inclusions appear as irregular cavities with foreign material embedded in them. Here is the reading trick for castings. Smooth and rounded features were shaped by liquid metal. Ragged and branched features were torn while the metal was solidifying. Shape tells you the mechanism, and location confirms it. Secondary manufacturing means working sound metal, hot or cold, by forging and rolling. A forging lap forms when a fold of surface metal gets pressed over and into the surface without fusing. On the part it appears as a smooth, gently curving line at a shallow angle to the surface, often near flash lines and corners. A forging burst is a tear that happens when metal is worked too cold or too fast. Most bursts are internal, which means visual testing alone will not find them unless machining exposes one. In rolled bar, look for seams. A seam is a tight, straight, longitudinal line that can run the full length of the bar, and it can be so tight that you need strong light at a low grazing angle to catch it. In plate, the classic problem is the lamination, a flat separation parallel to the surface. You will almost never see it on the plate face. You find it on cut edges, sheared edges, and drilled holes, where it shows as a thin dark line at mid thickness. So your habits for this lesson are simple. Ask what process made the part. Let the process tell you where to look. Read the shape of what you find, smooth versus ragged. Then read the address, heavy section versus section change versus edge. By the end of the practice exercise you should be able to take any common manufacturing discontinuity and place it correctly in the family tree, inherent, casting, or forging and rolling, and say why it belongs there.

sections.heading: Why Origin Matters
sections.body:
Visual testing at Level II is a prediction game. Before the part reaches your bench you should already know what its manufacturing route can produce, where each discontinuity likes to live, and what it looks like when it breaks the surface. A casting pulls your eyes to section-thickness changes and the metal under risers. A forging makes you walk the flash line. Rolled plate makes you check the cut edges before the faces. That mental map is what separates an examiner from a person with a flashlight.

Two terms first. A **discontinuity** is any interruption in the normal structure of the material. It becomes a **defect** only after evaluation shows it violates the acceptance criteria — that chain is the subject of a later lesson in this module. This lesson uses the CP-105 grouping: **inherent** discontinuities from the original melt and ingot, **primary manufacturing** discontinuities such as those in castings, and **secondary manufacturing** discontinuities such as those in forgings and rolled products. Other references slice the taxonomy slightly differently; what matters in the field is tying appearance to origin, because origin predicts location, orientation, and shape.

sections.heading: Inherent Discontinuities: Born in the Melt
sections.body:
Steel starts as a melt poured into an ingot mold or a continuous caster, and solidification is never perfectly clean. Four inherent discontinuities form here:

- **Pipe** — metal shrinks as it freezes, and the last liquid sits at the top center of the ingot. When feed metal runs out, a funnel-shaped cavity forms. Mills crop most of it, but remnants can survive into finished product.
- **Gas porosity** — dissolved gases come out of solution during freezing and leave smooth, rounded holes.
- **Nonmetallic inclusions** — slag, refractory, oxides, and sulfides trapped in the solidifying metal.
- **Segregation** — alloying elements concentrate where the metal freezes last, leaving bands of off-chemistry material that can etch, machine, and corrode differently than the surrounding metal.

The key Level II idea: inherent discontinuities are the raw material for later ones. Roll the ingot into plate and the pipe and porosity flatten into **laminations**; inclusions stretch into **stringers**; ingot surface defects become tight **seams**. The discontinuity you find on a plate edge today was born in a melt shop, possibly years ago. That is why mill certificates and material traceability matter to an inspector — pedigree predicts problems.

sections.caption: How inherent ingot discontinuities — pipe, gas porosity, inclusions, and segregation — become laminations, stringers, and seams when the ingot is rolled into product.

sections.heading: Casting Discontinuities (Primary Manufacturing)
sections.body:
A casting solidifies in its final shape, so metal flow and cooling control everything you will find:

- **Gas porosity** — smooth, round or slightly elongated holes with shiny walls, often just under the cope surface or exposed by the first machining cut. Rounded geometry says gas, not tearing.
- **Shrinkage** — jagged, spongy, dendritic cavities. It forms where metal freezes last: heavy sections, junctions where walls intersect, and directly under risers that could not feed enough liquid. Surface-breaking shrinkage looks like torn, crystalline sponge.
- **Cold shut** — two metal streams met after their fronts cooled too far to fuse. The result is a smooth, curved, linear indication with rounded edges, like a crease or fold. Expect it on thin walls far from the gate, where metal arrives coldest.
- **Misrun** — the mold never filled completely; the void has rounded, smooth edges.
- **Hot tear** — the mold restrained the casting while it was still hot and weak, tearing it. Ragged, branched, and almost always at a change in section thickness or a restrained corner.
- **Sand and slag inclusions** — irregular cavities with embedded foreign material, frequently on surfaces that faced loose mold material.

Also watch wall thickness: **core shift** thins one wall while thickening the opposite one, which you can sometimes catch visually at openings and machined bores.

sections.caption: A stepped casting in cross-section showing where shrinkage, gas porosity, hot tears, cold shuts, and sand inclusions typically form.

sections.title: Read the Shape, Then the Address
sections.body: **Smooth and rounded** features were formed by liquid metal — gas porosity, cold shuts, misruns. **Ragged, branched, or spongy** features were torn or starved during solidification — hot tears and shrinkage. Then confirm with location: shrinkage sits in heavy sections and under risers, hot tears at section changes and restrained corners, cold shuts on thin walls far from the gate. When shape and address agree, your interpretation is usually right.

sections.heading: Forging and Rolling Discontinuities (Secondary Manufacturing)
sections.body:
Secondary manufacturing works sound metal into shape — forging, rolling, drawing, extruding. Its discontinuities are mechanical, created by the deformation itself or by inherited material being smeared into new geometry:

- **Lap (forging)** — a fold of surface metal pressed over and into the part without fusing. It lies at a shallow angle to the surface and appears as a smooth, gently curving line, typically near flash lines, corners, and die-contact transitions. Because the metal folded rather than cracked, the edges look rolled and soft.
- **Burst (forging)** — an internal or surface tear produced by forging at too low a temperature or reducing too aggressively. Internal bursts are invisible to VT; surface bursts appear as ragged tears, and one exposed burst is a strong argument for volumetric testing of the whole lot.
- **Seam (rolling)** — an ingot surface crack or fold rolled into a tight, straight, longitudinal line. Seams can run the full bar length and be nearly closed — use strong light at a low grazing angle and a 10x magnifier to confirm.
- **Lamination (rolling)** — pipe, porosity, or inclusions flattened into a planar separation parallel to the plate surface. The plate face shows nothing; the discontinuity reveals itself on sheared or flame-cut edges and inside drilled holes as a thin dark mid-thickness line.

Hydrogen flakes — internal hairline cracks in heavy forgings — round out the list, but like internal bursts they belong to volumetric methods.

sections.caption: Four secondary-processing signatures — lap, burst, seam, and lamination — and the geometry that gives each one away.
Manufacturing discontinuity quick reference.
rows: Heavy sections, wall junctions, under risers
Thin walls far from the gate
Smooth fold at a shallow angle to the surface
Machined surfaces that may expose one
Bar and tube OD, full length, grazing light
Cut or sheared edges, drilled holes
Machined surfaces, parallel to rolling direction

figures.title: Inherent Discontinuities: Ingot to Wrought Product
Forging and Rolling Discontinuities: Four Signatures

interactive.title: Place It in the Family Tree
interactive.intro: Classify each discontinuity by its origin: inherent from the original melt and ingot, primary manufacturing in castings, or secondary manufacturing in forging and rolling. Think origin, not where you happened to find it.

items.label: Ingot pipe
items.why: Pipe is the funnel-shaped shrinkage cavity at the top center of a solidifying ingot — the classic inherent discontinuity.

items.label: Nonmetallic inclusion stringers
items.why: The inclusions entered the metal in the melt; rolling only elongated them into stringers. Origin decides the family.

items.label: Segregation
items.why: Alloying elements concentrate in the last ingot regions to freeze — a chemistry condition born in solidification of the melt.

items.label: Cold shut
items.why: Two casting streams met too cold to fuse. Only liquid metal flowing in a mold can create one.

items.label: Hot tear
items.why: The mold restrained the casting while it was hot and weak, tearing it at a section change — a casting-process event.

items.label: Shrinkage cavity under a riser
items.why: Last-to-freeze casting metal ran out of feed liquid — jagged, dendritic, and located exactly where casting theory predicts.

items.label: Forging lap
items.why: A lap is folded in by the dies during forging. Working sound metal is secondary manufacturing.

items.label: Forging burst
items.why: Bursts are tears from working metal too cold or reducing it too fast — a forging-process error.

items.label: Lamination found on a plate edge
items.why: Rolling flattened ingot pipe, porosity, or inclusions into a planar separation — the rolled product carries it, even though the melt seeded it.

checkQuestions.question: During VT of a sand casting you find a smooth, curved linear indication with rounded edges on a thin wall far from the gate. What is the most likely discontinuity?
options: Hot tear
Shrinkage cavity
Cold shut
Forging lap
checkQuestions.explanation: Smooth, rounded, fold-like geometry means two metal fronts met too cold to fuse, and thin walls far from the gate are where metal arrives coldest — the classic cold shut signature. A lap is a forging discontinuity, not a casting one.

checkQuestions.question: Where should you concentrate your search for shrinkage in a casting?
options: At the last regions to solidify — heavy sections, wall junctions, and under risers
Along the parting line only
On thin walls farthest from the riser
At the gate where metal enters the mold
checkQuestions.explanation: Shrinkage forms where metal freezes last and runs out of feed liquid, which is the heavy sections, junctions, and the metal directly under risers.

checkQuestions.question: Why is a lamination in rolled plate rarely detected on the plate face during VT?
options: It is too small to resolve with normal visual acuity
It only forms after the plate enters service
Laminations always weld themselves shut during rolling
It lies parallel to the plate surface, so it only shows where an edge, hole, or cut crosses it
checkQuestions.explanation: A lamination is a planar separation parallel to the rolled surfaces. VT can only see it where that plane is intersected — cut edges, sheared edges, and drilled holes.

checkQuestions.question: Which statement best describes a forging lap?
options: A ragged internal tear from forging the metal too cold
Surface metal folded over and pressed in without fusing, lying at a shallow angle to the surface
A funnel-shaped cavity at the top of an ingot
A network of fine surface cracks from temperature cycling
checkQuestions.explanation: A lap is a fold of surface metal pressed into the part without fusion. It sits at a shallow angle to the surface and reads as a smooth, gently curving line — the ragged internal tear describes a burst.

checkQuestions.question: Stringers found on a machined surface of rolled bar trace back to which original condition?
options: Quench cracking during heat treatment
Abrasive wear in service
Nonmetallic inclusions in the original melt, elongated by rolling
Undercut from a welding operation
checkQuestions.explanation: Stringers are inherent nonmetallic inclusions that the rolling operation stretched out along the grain direction; machining simply exposes them.

references: CP-105-2024 VT Level II Topical Outline §7.1, §7.2, §7.4
ASTM E1316, Standard Terminology for Nondestructive Examinations (discontinuity terms)
General casting and wrought-product discontinuity atlases (industry references, background reading)
